article_title: Tariffs and AI Memory Demand Converge to Drive PC Prices Higher as Sub-$600 Laptops Disappear From Shelves

content_preview.title: Tariffs and AI Memory Demand Converge to Drive PC Prices Higher as Sub-$600 Laptops Disappear From Shelves
content_preview.summary: One year after Liberation Day, the collision of Trump-era tariffs with hyperscaler memory hoarding has pushed PC prices up 15-20 percent, forced vendors to exit budget segments, and reshaped the consumer electronics supply chain.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## The Squeeze on Consumer Hardware

The sub-$600 laptop is becoming an endangered species. Major PC vendors including Lenovo, Dell, HP, Acer, and ASUS have confirmed [price increases of 15 to 20 percent across their product lines](https://www.tomshardware.com/desktops/gaming-pcs/idc-slashes-2026-pc-shipment-forecast-amid-memory-shortages-total-pc-market-value-to-nonetheless-increase-to-usd274-billion-due-to-ongoing-price-hikes), with entry-level systems absorbing the steepest specification down...

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong analysis of tariff+memory convergence driving consumer electronics prices.
editor_notes.source_verification: CFR, Newsweek, Tom's Hardware — all authoritative.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Sourced from policy analysis and tech reporting.
editor_notes.originality: Distinct from Section 232 tariff article — focuses on tech-specific consumer impact.
